coredata.dc:title: Degradation of acid blue 41 with carbon quantum dots@MOF-808 nanocomposite as a biocompatible photocatalyst under visible light

coredata.dc:description: CQDs@MOF-808 guest@host nanocomposite as a biocompatible photocatalyst has been synthesized using a ship-bottle approach involving the impregnation method for immobilization of glucose molecules as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) precursor into the MOF-808 pores to form Glucose@MOF-808 and further fabrication of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via in-situ thermolysis route of glucose molecules loaded into the pores of MOF-808. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of 86.62 % was calculated for CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite for degradation of Acid Blue 41 in 120 min under visible light irradiation, that was attributed to the presence of CQDs inside MOF-808 pores. The exceptional characteristics of CQDs include outstanding up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL) effects, photo-induced electron transfer, electron reservoir properties, and electron-hole separations, all contributing to the photocatalytic degradation of Acid Blue 41. The synthesized samples were characterized by PXRD, FT-IR, EDX, N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm, TEM, DRS, PL, EIS and Mott − Sckottky measurements.

head.ce:title: Degradation of acid blue 41 with carbon quantum dots@MOF-808 nanocomposite as a biocompatible photocatalyst under visible light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CQDs@MOF-808 guest@host nanocomposite as a biocompatible photocatalyst has been synthesized using a ship-bottle approach involving the impregnation method for immobilization of glucose molecules as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) precursor into the MOF-808 pores to form Glucose@MOF-808 and further fabrication of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via in-situ thermolysis route of glucose molecules loaded into the pores of MOF-808. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of 86.62 % was calculated for CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite for degradation of Acid Blue 41 in 120 min under visible light irradiation, that was attributed to the presence of CQDs inside MOF-808 pores. The exceptional characteristics of CQDs include outstanding up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL) effects, photo-induced electron transfer, electron reservoir properties, and electron-hole separations, all contributing to the photocatalytic degradation of Acid Blue 41. The synthesized samples were characterized by PXRD, FT-IR, EDX, N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm, TEM, DRS, PL, EIS and Mott − Sckottky measurements.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Global industrialization has caused significant threats and damages to both humans and the environment by introducing various contaminants into the air, water, and soil. Water is often contaminated by industrial waste, such as dyes, pharmaceuticals, phenolic compounds, insecticides, pesticides, heavy metals, and other harmful substances. Despite their low concentrations, these pollutants are considered harmful to human health and the environment. Therefore, it's essential to treat wastewater from these industries before releasing it into natural water sources. Wastewater can be treated in various ways, including adsorption, mechanical vapor recompression, precipitation, biodegradation, solvent extraction, membrane separation, ion exchange, chemical oxidation, coagulation, sonochemical elimination, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), photocatalysis, and more. The significant impact on environmental pollution and the global energy issue has made photocatalysis a popular choice among them. Photocatalysis is a low-cost, efficient, and environmentally friendly technique that uses light instead of chemicals to remove hazardous pollutants [1–3]. Photocatalysts can be divided into two main categories: traditional photocatalysts, which include ZnO, TiO2, BiVO4, Fe2O3, WO3, Cu2O, Ag3PO4, and CdS, and emerging photocatalysts such as metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), quantum dots (metal oxide QDs, metal sulfide QDs, carbon dots (CQDs), and graphene dots (GQDs)), two-dimensional materials (black P, g-C3N4, BiOX, and MoS2), and plasmonic metals (Ag, Au, Al, and Bi). Photocatalysts, both traditional and modified, have been used for photocatalytic applications because to their non-toxicity, chemical and thermal stability, and low cost. However, this system has several drawbacks, such as inadequate adsorption, the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs, and the inflexible structure, which ultimately limits their applicability in the field of photocatalysis [4]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with particle sizes below 10 nm which have the electronic properties of carbon materials and the optical properties of quantum dots [5–7]. The carbon nanoparticles were first discovered in 2004 by Yan et al during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes, and then, in 2006, they were called “carbon quantum dots” by Sun et al [8,9]. The green synthesis of CQDs involves using renewable resources, as well as low-cost and non-toxic raw materials. Various organic and inorganic materials can be used to construct CQDs, including citric acid, ascorbic acid, ammonium citrate, graphite, carbohydrates, cholesterol, glucose, gelatin, lignin, as well as biomass resources, such as plant parts, fruits, vegetables, spices, animal derivatives, and natural polymers, by utilizing different synthesis techniques such as solvothermal, hydrothermal, sonochemical, laser ablation, microwave irradiation, electrochemical oxidation, pyrolysis, and thermolysis [10,11]. While CQD synthesis is simple, aggregation and size control at the quantum scale are challenges [12]. CQDs have recently been receiving considerable attention due to their applications in bioimaging, nanomedicine, drug delivery, photodynamic therapy, chemical sensing, solar cells, energy storage, light-emitting diodes, electrocatalysis, and photocatalysis, owing to their quantum confinement effect, strong optical properties, biocompatibility, low toxicity, good solubility in water, high photostability, and low-cost synthesis [5,9–13]. A variety of unique properties of CQDs make them valuable for developing photocatalysts, including excellent sunlight harvesting, tunable photoluminescence (PL), and up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL). CQDs can be used as a sole photocatalyst or as a co-catalyst to improve the performance of other photocatalysts, such as adsorption centers, spectrum converters, photosensitizers, electron mediators, and electron reservoirs [6,8,11,13,14]. Due to their high surface-to-volume ratio, CQDs can serve as adsorption centers, enhancing photocatalytic degradation through interfacial adhesion [11]. In CQD-based hybrid systems, CQDs can be used as spectral converters due to upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) effect converts incident light into high energy emitted light, allowing the full spectrum of sunlight to be utilized by photocatalysts. CQDs act as a photosensitizer instead of a valence band to provide electrons to the conduction band of semiconductor. CQDs can serve as the electron mediator to retard the recombination of electron-hole pairs; CQDs can function as electron reservoirs to trap electrons generated to improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers [6,8,12–14]. Due to their solubility in water, CQDs cannot be recovered after the photocatalytic process, resulting in environmental pollution [11,14]. A number of porous materials are proposed as templates and supports for immobilizing and preparing nanosized photocatalysts and catalysts on/into solid supports to prevent aggregation and leakage, including zeolites, diatomite, mesoporous silica, porous carbons, carbon nanotubes, graphene, polymers, and metal–organic frameworks [15–18]. For instance, Eghbali et al. effectively immobilized a molybdenum-glycine Schiff base complex within the nanocages of zeolite Y using the flexible ligand technique. The MoO2glySchiff@Y material was used in the epoxidation of olefins using tert-butylhydroperoxide as an oxidant [17]. Furthermore, SBA-16 is a mesoporous silica that SBA-16 nanocages were utilized to synthesize and immobilize amino acid Schiff base complexes of molybdenum by Masteri-Farahani et al. The prepared heterogeneous catalysts were utilized for the epoxidation of olefins using tert-butyl hydroperoxide (TBHP) [18]. As emerging porous materials, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are composed of metal units and organic ligands. Due to their high crystallinity, large surface areas, adjustable pore sizes, high porosity, templating effects, and host–guest properties, they make excellent materials for drug delivery, ion exchange, sensors, and antibacterial applications catalysis, gas storage/separation and photocatalysis [4,19–34]. Recent research has focused on the templating effects of MOFs, including template-directed synthesis and using MOFs as a template for the formation of guest species into pores [4,15,32–39]. The MOFs exhibit unique host–guest properties thanks to free space like channels or polyhedral cages and tunable pore environments via post-synthetic modification or assembling or one-pot synthesis. To improve MOFs' photocatalytic activity, they can be used as templates to synthesize metals, metal sulfides, metal oxides, molecular species, polyoxometalates, and quantum dots [4,15,32–34,36–42]. As a result of their high porosity and adjustable pore sizes, MOFs offer an ideal encapsulation environment for QDs and prevent them from aggregating. Today, QDs@MOFs guest@host nanocomposite as advanced materials is a hot topic in the nanoworld because of improved physicochemical and optical–electrical properties than single QDs and MOFs that are considered a promising photocatalyst in the future. The QDs@MOFs guest@host nanocomposite can be fabricated using photodeposition, direct surface functionalization, the ship-bottle and bottle-ship approaches. QDs can be deposited onto MOFs surfaces through photochemical deposition, which requires photo-reducing metallic precursors with enough redox potential. Another approach to suspending QDs on MOF interfaces is through direct surface functionalization. A ship-bottle approach involves loading QDs precursors in the pores of MOFs via impregnation, double solution, chemical vapor infiltration, and then pyrolysis, thermolysis or reduction methods to synthesize QDs, whereas the bottle-ship approach starts with QDs synthesized after MOF precursors have been added, which causes MOF to form around the QDs in order to fabricated QDs@MOFs nanocomposites. Smart integration of QDs into a MOF template results in improved physico-chemical properties for MOFs [34,43,44]. The CQDs@MOFs nanocomposites such as CDs/NH2-MIL-125(Ti) [45], NH2-MIL-88B(Fe)/CDs [46] and MOF-808/S,N-CQDs [47] have been utilized for the photocatalytic degradation of dye pollutants. The preparation of composites based on MOF-808 with sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots(S,N-CQDs) onits surface has been reportedbyRahmanietal. In this work, two precursors, citric acid (CA) and thiourea (TU), were used to prepare S,N-CQDs. MOF-808 composites with S,N-CQDs were made by utilizing a solvent-deposition technique. The MOF-808/S,N-CQDs(x) (MS-x) composites were used to degrade RhB. It was found that MS-0.5 composite had the most effective photocatalytic activity, removing 89 % of RhB under visible light and 95 % under sunlight illumination [47].

Recent advancements in dye technology have led to the creation of many new synthetic dyes. These artificial dyes are now widely used in various industries, including textile production, medicine, and cosmetics. There is a significant amount of dye wastewater that comes with it. Approximately 100 tons of dye effluent is generated annually worldwide. The composition of dye wastewater is highly intricate, with the majority of them containing recalcitrant organic substances, high chroma, and persistent pollution. Due to the obvious color changes during dye degradation, MOFs-based photocatalysts have made many advances in the degradation of dyes [48]. Table 1 summarizes the photocatalytic degradation of dyes by the MOFs composites.

In this work, MOF-808 with high porosity, large pore size (1.8 nm), chemical stability, thermal stability, and biocompatibility [20,58,59] was used as a template to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs) inside its pores. Glucose molecules (size 0.9 nm) were loaded into the pores to form a Glucose@MOF-808 guest@host nanocomposite, and then carbon quantum dots were synthesized within the same pores via an in-situ thermolysis route. The resulting CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity for degrading Acid Blue 41 under visible light due to the properties of the carbon quantum dots.

Zirconium(IV) chloride (ZrCl4((Merck, 98 %), 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC) (Merck, 95 %), Glucose (Glu) (Merck, 97.5 %), Silver nitrate (AgNO3) (Merck, 99.8 %), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) (Merck, 99.8 %), Formic acid (FA) (Merck, 98 %), Hydrochloric acid (HCl) (Merck, 37 %), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) (Merck, 99 %), Ethanol (Merck, 99.8 %), Methanol (Merck, 99.6 %), Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (Merck, 35 %), Buffer solution (pH=7), Distilled water (H2O) and Acid Blue 41 was obtained from Alvan Sabet company (Iran). All chemicals were purchased from commercial suppliers and used as received without further purification.

Zirconium(IV) chloride (5 mmol, 1.15 g) and 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (5 mmol, 1.05 g) were dissolved in a mixture of DMF and formic acid (50 mL/50 mL) and refluxed for 24 h at 100 °C. After synthesizing MOF-808, it was washed several times with DMF and methanol. In order to exchange the guest solvent molecules, powder of MOF-808 was immersed in 50 mL of methanol for three d, which was replaced overtime per d and activated at 155 °C for 24 h. Synthesis procedure of MOF-808 was based on literature [20,21,60].

Activated MOF-808 (0.300 g) was dispersed in a glucose solution (0.500 g glucose dissolved in 50 mL water) over 1 h. The mixture was then sonicated for 44 min at 70 °C and immersed for 24 h. The mixture was filtered and cleaned with H2O and dried in the air. So, a glucose@MOF-808 guest@host nanocomposites is then formed through the impregnation method.

The CQDs@MOF-808 guest@host nanocomposite was prepared by in-situ thermolysis of glucose@MOF-808 at 200 °C with a heating rate of 3 °C·min−1. The temperature was held at 200 °C for 30 min. The conversion of glucose into carbon quantum dots led to the formation of CQDs@MOF-808 guest@host nanocomposite.

The instruments used to characterize the MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808 were an X-Ray Diffractometer (Equinox 3000, Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.541874 Å)), fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (AVATAR) with KBr pellets as a reference, field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM EM-8000), transmission electron microscopy (TEM Philips EM 208S), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) (BELSORP MINI II), UV–Visible diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (DRS) (AVASPEC-ULS3648-RS) using BaSO4 as reference material, fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian Carry Eclips, excitation at 210 nm), Potentiostat/Galvanostat (IVIUM Vertex), Zeta potential analyzer (SZ-100z), ICP-OES spectrometer (ARCOS), and Rayleigh spectrophotometer (UV-2601). The gas chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (GC System: 7890A / Mass Selective Detector: 5975C VL MSD with Triple-Axis Detector) and Mass Spectrometer (G708 1B MSD with Tripe-Axis Detector) were used to identify the by-products after degradation.

Potentiostat/Galvanostat (IVIUM Vertex) was used to perform Mott-Schottky analysis and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using saturated calomel electrodes (SCE), platinum disks (Pt), and glassy carbon electrodes for reference, counter, and working electrodes, respectively. For the preparation of working electrodes, 3 mg of the sample was dispersed in 100 µL of ethanol using an ultrasonic bath for 10 min. 3 µL of the dispersed mixture was dripped onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with a diameter of 2 mm as a working electrode and let to dry under an IR lamp. Then 2 µL of Nafion solution was added to the surface and allowed to dry by the same method. Under dark conditions, the Mott-Schottky measurement was conducted with an amplitude of 10 mV, a frequency of 1000 Hz and an EIS test at an open-circuit potential at a frequency of 0.1–––1000000 Hz, amplitude of 0.05 V.

In order to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the samples, an aqueous solution of Acid Blue 41 was used as the model dye pollutant with a high-pressure Mercury-vapor lamp as the radiation source (visible light at wavelength > 400 nm, 400 W, 3.25 A, intensity 23,200 Lm). In 50 mL of Acid Blue 41 aqueous solution (40 ppm), 10 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed. To ensure adsorption–desorption equilibrium between Acid Blue 41 and photocatalyst, the suspension was magnetically stirred in the dark for 60 min. After adding 0.2 mL of H2O2, the solution was exposed to visible light (λ > 400 nm) irradiation under magnetic stirring. The suspension was immediately centrifuged every 60 min after 3 mL aliquots were taken. The concentration of Acid Blue 41 was measured at λmax = 605 nm with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

In comparison to the simulated MOF-808 [60], PXRD confirmed the successful synthesis of MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite (Fig. 1 ). The PXRD pattern of CQDs@MOF-808 confirmed that after in situ thermolysis of glucose in MOF-808 to form CQDs in it at 200 °C, the host framework crystallinity was preserved due to its thermal stability. The PXRD pattern of CQDs@MOF-808 showed a new peak at 27.46°, suggesting the formation of CQDs into MOF-808 which is in agreement with that reported in literature [61]. The results proved that CQDs are immobilized into MOF-808, and CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite is successfully synthesized. The average crystallite size (D) and cell parameter (a) were calculated for three main reflections (311), (222) and (400) from the PXRD patterns of MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808 using equations (1) and (2), respectively [62]. The average crystallite sizes of MOF-808 and the CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite were determined to be 20.87 nm and 21.05 nm, respectively. The cell parameters (a) for MOF-808 and the CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposites were 34.98 and 34.44 Å, respectively. The CQDs were formed within the pores of the MOF-808 framework during heating and resulted in subtle changes to the nanocomposites. A slight increase in the average size of crystal particles resulted in a slight decrease in the distance between atoms in the crystal structure. As a result, a slight decrease in the cell parameter of the CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite was observed.

MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808 exhibit similar vibration bands in their FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 ). The vibration peak at 658 cm−1 was attributed to Zr-O, and peaks around 1383, 1581, and 1617 cm−1 were corresponded to the stretching modes of carboxylate groups coordinated with Zr6 clusters [63,64]. The difference between MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808 reflected in the vibration peaks at 1183 cm−1 (C=O bond), and 1709 cm−1 (C-O bond) that were attributed to COOH groups in CQDs [65]. Therefore, FT-IR results confirm that the CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite was successfully synthesized.

CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite components have been investigated using EDX. According to Fig. 3 (A), CQDs@MOF-808 contain Zr, C and O elements. EDX mapping was used to map the Zr, C and O distribution in CQDs@MOF-808 (Fig. 3(B)) which further confirms the successful synthesis of CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite. The Au peaks are due to the addition of a thin layer of gold to CQDs@MOF-808 in order to perform EDX mapping analysis.

In Fig. 4 , the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms for MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808 were shown. Type-I isotherm shapes indicate their microporous characteristics. MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808 had BET surface areas of 1094.1 m2.g−1 and 827.3 m2.g−1, and their pore volumes were 1.20 cm3.g−1 and 0.67 cm3.g−1, respectively. As compared to the pristine MOF-808, the observed decreases in adsorbed N2, BET surface area, and pore volume should be attributed to the formation of CQDs as guest species inside pores of the MOF-808 host via in situ thermolysis route of glucose. Also embedding of CQDs into MOF-808 pores resulted in a decrease in pore diameter from 4.39 nm to 3.26 nm for CQDs@MOF-808.The results showed that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were formed within the pores of the MOF-808 host as a template for the synthesis of CQDs@MOF-808 guest@host nanocomposites.

Based on Fig. 5 .A, CQDs@MOF-808 guest@host nanocomposite has spherical shape with approximately 50 nm diameter. It has a smooth outer surface without agglomerations of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5.B, dispersed CQDs were found within MOF-808 which indicates that CQDs were with the size of about 1.5 nm encapsulated within the pores of MOF-808.

The absorbance spectra of MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808 are shown in Fig. 6 .A. The DRS spectrum of MOF-808 exhibited two absorption bands at 244 nm and 286 nm, which could be assigned to the π − π* transitions of 1,3,5-benzene tricarboxylate ligand and ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) of O(II) => Zr(IV) in MOF-808, respectively. After, controllable synthesis of CQDs via in situ thermolysis route of the glucose into the pores of MOF-808, the absorbance spectrum of CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite showed two new absorption peaks at 340 nm and 405 which can be attributed to the electronic transition of π − π* and n − π* in CQDs [61]. The absorption edge of CQDs@MOF-808 was red-shifted to the visible light region in comparison with MOF-808 and as a result CQDs@MOF-808 is photoactive to visible light. The band gap energy of MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite have been calculated using the tauc-plot technique (αhν) n = A (hν − Eg) [66] with values of 4.05 eV and 2.55 eV, respectively (Fig. 6.B). The results are in good agreement with previous reports [61,67,68].

Fig. 7 illustrates the photoluminescence analysis of MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808. Due to the formation of CQDs into the pores of MOF-808, the PL spectrum of CQDs@MOF-808 has lower intensities than MOF-808. CQDs@MOF-800, as a visible-light-driven guest@host nanocomposite, showed excellent photocatalytic activity for the photodegradation of Acid Blue 41.

In order to study the charge transfer resistance of MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808, EIS technique was used. The EIS spectrum of CQDs@MOF-808 shows the smallest semicircle compared to MOF-808, confirming that CQDs@MOF-808 possess the lowest interfacial electron transfer resistance and high electronic conductivity (Fig. 8 ).

The Mott − Schottky (M−S) analysis was used for the calculation of the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) potentials of MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808 and the effect of the CQDs on the band structure of the MOF-808. As displayed in Fig. 9 , the flat-band potentials (Efb) of MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808 are about 0.06 and 0.12 eV vs. SCE (0.30 and 0.36 eV vs. NHE [69]), respectively. The curves havea positive slope thatreveals their typical n-type semiconductivity. For n-type semiconductors the conduction band potential (ECB) is about 0.10 V above the flat-band potential [70]. Therefore, the conduction band (ECB) potentials were calculated as 0.20 and 0.26 eV vs. NHE for MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808, respectively. Based on the empirical formula: EVB=Eg + ECB [69] and the band gap and conduction band potential values, the valence band (VB) potentials of MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808 are calculated to be 4.25 and 2.81 eV vs. NHE, respectively. The results of the M−S analysis showed that the formation of CQDs into MOF-808 led to a positive and negative shift in the conduction and valence band edge, and the band gap energy of MOF-808 decreased from 4.05 to 2.55 eV. As a result, the CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite was applied as a visible light-responsive photocatalyst with high activity for photodegradation of Acid Blue 41 under visible light.

The control experiments were performed under different conditions to ensure the photocatalytic activity of MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808. Fig. 10 . A shows the concentration changes (Ct/Ci) of Acid Blue 41 over time under different conditions. A absorption efficiency [71] of 14.28 % and 57.08 % were calculated for MOF-808 and CQDs@MOF-808 under dark conditions for 180 min, respectively. Compared to MOF-808, CQDs@MOF-808 displayed superior absorption capacity because of the π–π stacking interaction [11] between the conjugated sp2 carbon structure of CQDs and the aromatic rings of Acid Blue 41. Photocatalytic degradation of Acid Blue 41 under visible-light radiation was studied using a pseudo-first-order model (ln(C0/Ct) = -kt) [72]. In Fig. 10.B the linearity of C0/Ct is observed through the graph of ln (C0/Ct) versus t. The kinetic rate constants are shown in Fig. 10.C, the self- photolysis of Acid Blue 41 was almost negligible. It is estimated that CQDs@MOF-808 displayed the highest rate constant (0.01676 min−1), which is approximately 1.65 and 2.95 times higher than MOF=808 (0.01012 min−1) and H2O2 (0.00568 min−1). Fig. 10.D shows the absorption changes of Acid Blue 41 aqueous solution in the presence of CQDs@MOF-808. The blue color of Acid Blue 41 solution disappeared, after irradiated by visible light for 120 min. After 120 min, the degradation yield did not significantly change, so the retention time [73] was determined as 120 min. This improvement in photocatalytic activity of CQDs@MOF-808 can be attributed to the presence of CQDs, which can function as absorption centers, high up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL) effects, electron reservoirs, electron-hole separations, and narrowing MOF-808′s band gap. The results of this work were compared with similar works in Table 2 . The stability of CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite was confirmed via ICP-OES analysis that investigated the leached metal concentration of Zr. The concentration of Zr metal was 335000 ppm before the photocatalyst, but it was reduced to 323800 ppm afterward. The amount of leached Zr was found to be 3.48 % after photocatalysis, indicating the good stability of the CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite. The pH of the zero point of charge (pHZPC) is defined as the pH at which the surface charge of a material is zero. In buffer solution, the pHzpc of CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite was determined to be 7 (pHzpc = 7). The surface charge of CQDs@MOF-808 is neutral at pH=7, while it is positive at pH below 7 and negative at pH higher than 7. The molecular structure of Acid Blue 41 is sensitive to pH. The molecular structure of this dye contains an azo group. The color of it changes in response to pH environments. In acidic environments, the azo group is in a protonated state and appears blue. As the pH increases and the environment becomes alkaline, protons are removed from the azo group and the color changes to its neutral form. This neutral form is red. With a further increase in pH and a more alkaline environment, the neutral form of Acid Blue 41 also becomes colorless. This phenomenon occurs due to the opening of the benzthiazole ring in the molecular structure of the Acid Blue 41.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism ofPhotocatalytic degradation of CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite's enhanced photocatalytic activity can be explained as follows (Fig. 11 ): MOF-808 with a wide band gap of 4.05 eV cannot absorb visible light. The CQDs as a co-catalyst can transfer visible light (λ > 400 nm) with longer wavelength into UV light (λ < 400 nm) via the up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL) effects. MOF-808 generates electron-hole pairs as a result of being excited by UV light. CQDs, acting as electron reservoirs, can trap electrons from the MOF-808 conduction band, inhibiting charge recombination and extending charge carrier lifetimes. Electrons (e-) react with H2O2, and holes (h+) react with water (H2O) to form hydroxyl radicals (•OH) [32–34], which degrade Acid Blue 41. The Acid Blue 41 can be directly oxidized by MOF-808 via long-lived holes (h+) in its valence band. Reactive species trapping experiments of CQDs@MOF-808 nanocomposite confirmed the proposed mechanism. The p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), isopropanol (IPA), ethanol (EtOH), and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were used as scavenging species of •O2 –, •OH, h+, and e-, respectively. As shown in Fig. 12 , adding p-BQ does not affect the photocatalytic degradation of Acid Blue 41. The addition of IPA, EtOH, and AgNO3 has a significant impact on the decrease in photocatalytic degradation of Acid Blue 41. The AgNO3 has prevented the formation of •OH through electron trapping, which has led to a decrease in degradation efficiency. In this study, it was found that the main reactive species for photocatalytic degradation of Acid Blue 41 are •OH and h+. The GC/MS and MS techniques were used to identify the by-products produced after degradation. The GC–MS library database verified that acetaldehyde (m/z = 44) and propanamide (m/z = 73) were formed after Acid Blue 41 degradation.

The CQDs@MOF-808 guest@host nanocomposite as a biocompatible photocatalyst was successfully designed and synthesized using the ship-bottle approach. The glucose@MOF-808 guest@host nanocomposite was prepared by loading glucose molecules as CQD precursors in the MOF-808 pores. After that, CQDs embedded in the MOF-808 pores were formed using an in-situ thermolysis route. PXRD, FT-IR, EDX, and TEM analyses confirmed the presence of CDQs in MOF-808. Compared to the pristine MOF-808, CQDs@MOF-808 had a BET surface area of 827.3 m2.g−1 and a pore volume of 0.67 cm3.g−1, indicating carbon quantum dots (CQDs)were formed into the pores of the MOF-808host as a template. As a result of the guest@host interactions in CQDs@MOF-808, the MOF-808′s absorption edge to be red-shifted from the ultraviolet to the visible region and the band gap was decreased from 4.05 eV to 2.55 eV. In 120 min, CQDs@MOF-808 degraded Acid Blue 41 with 86.62 % photocatalytic efficiency thanks to the excellent UCPL, photo-induced electron transfer, and electron reservoir properties of CQDs.

Roghayyeh Ghasemzadeh: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Kamran Akhbari: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
